chunk_summary: This section evaluates vitamin A, zinc, and iodine deficiencies in vegetarian-fed children, emphasizing supplementation schedules, dietary strategies, and clinical interventions to address risks in Indian regional feeding patterns.
learning_objectives: Understand the national vitamin A supplementation schedule and its application in vegetarian-fed children.
Identify zinc supplementation guidelines for acute diarrhea and undernourished children.
Evaluate iodine intake adequacy and troubleshoot common dietary deficiencies in vegetarian diets.
key_facts: Vitamin A prophylaxis starts at 9 months with 100,000 IU and continues with 200,000 IU doses for children aged 1–5 years.
Zinc supplementation is recommended for acute diarrhea: 10 mg/day for children <6 months and 20 mg/day for children ≥6 months for 14 days.
Zinc supplementation is not recommended routinely but may be used in undernourished children with recurrent diarrhea or poor growth.
Iodine adequacy should be ensured through iodized salt; non-iodized rock salt or boutique salts are common causes of deficiency.
Iodine supplements should only be used with clear indications and a plan to prevent excess intake.
reference_quote: Vitamin A: integrate the national supplementation schedule into routine visits, especially when diet diversity is low. Prophylaxis commonly starts at 9 months (100,000 IU) and continues with 200,000 IU doses in the 1–5 year range to complete the program schedule.
document_key_topics: Vitamin A supplementation in vegetarian-fed children
Zinc supplementation for diarrhea and growth issues
